questions.subject: Dermatology
questions.stem: A 28-year-old has well-demarcated erythematous plaques with silvery scale on the elbows and knees, plus nail pitting. Scratching the scale leaves pinpoint bleeding. What is the diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Classic chronic plaque psoriasis: extensor distribution, silvery scale, nail pitting, and the Auspitz sign (pinpoint bleeding on scale removal).

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Eczema favours flexures, is itchy and ill-defined, with weeping/lichenification rather than silvery scale.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: LP = the 6 Ps (purple, polygonal, pruritic, planar papules) with Wickham's striae, typically on flexor wrists.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Herald patch then a 'fir-tree' truncal eruption that self-resolves — no silvery extensor plaques.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: An annular plaque with central clearing and a scaly advancing edge; KOH shows hyphae.

questions.keyPoint: Psoriasis = extensor surfaces + silvery scale + nail changes + Auspitz sign. Eczema is the flexural, itchy mirror image.
questions.keyDifferentiator: Psoriasis → extensor, silvery, well-defined. Eczema → flexural, itchy, ill-defined.

questions.subject: Dermatology
questions.stem: A 50-year-old has flaccid blisters that rupture into painful erosions, with significant oral mucosal involvement. Lateral pressure on normal skin shears the epidermis (Nikolsky positive). Which is the diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Intra-epidermal split (desmoglein antibodies) → flaccid blisters, prominent oral lesions, Nikolsky positive. Potentially fatal — needs systemic immunosuppression.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Sub-epidermal (at the DEJ) → TENSE blisters in the elderly, mucosa usually spared, Nikolsky NEGATIVE.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Intensely itchy grouped vesicles on extensors, linked to coeliac disease — not flaccid oral bullae.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Drug-related, acute, with target lesions and mucosal sloughing over days — a distinct emergency.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Inherited mechanobullous disorders presenting typically from birth/childhood.

questions.keyPoint: Pemphigus = intra-epidermal, flaccid, oral, Nikolsky POSITIVE, dangerous. Pemphigoid = sub-epidermal (DEJ), tense, elderly, Nikolsky NEGATIVE.
questions.keyDifferentiator: 'P-emphigus = P-ainful & oral, flaccid, Nikolsky +.' Pemphigoid = tense bullae, mucosa spared, Nikolsky −.

questions.subject: Dermatology
questions.stem: A child has an annular, scaly plaque with central clearing and a raised advancing edge on the trunk. KOH microscopy shows branching hyphae. What is the first-line treatment?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Localised tinea corporis (a dermatophyte, confirmed by KOH hyphae) responds to a topical antifungal such as terbinafine.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: A steroid alone worsens/masks dermatophytes ('tinea incognito') — avoid.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Antiviral — wrong organism (hyphae indicate a fungus, not a virus).

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: An antibacterial for localised impetigo — not antifungal.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Reserved for extensive disease or scalp/nail involvement, not a single localised body plaque.

questions.keyPoint: Annular plaque + central clearing + KOH hyphae = dermatophyte (tinea). Localised disease → topical terbinafine; never a steroid alone.
questions.keyDifferentiator: KOH hyphae → dermatophyte (antifungal). KOH 'spaghetti & meatballs' → pityriasis versicolor. Steroid alone = tinea incognito.
